entry.title: SAP Mobile Start: A comprehensive demo session
entry.content:
Imagine your customer’s workforce opening one app… and everything they need is just there.  Approvals.  Tasks.  Insights.  No hunting.  No switching between tools.

That’s the idea behind SAP Mobile Start: A comprehensive demo session, and we’re excited to show you what it looks like in action.

In this live session, we’ll break down the core functions, share real-life use cases, and talk honestly about the benefits of a “launchpad app for employees”.  The thing is that mobility is no longer a nice-to-have.  It’s the expectation.

We’ll also dive into architecture and technical requirements, so you leave not just inspired, but equipped.  You’ll understand where it fits, how it works, and what’s needed to implement it successfully.

If you’re advising customers on modern workplace strategies, this one matters.

Register now and let’s rethink mobile together.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/feb/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013129.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPMobileStart #FutureOfWork #SAPTechnology #DigitalTransformation

entry.title: Part 1: Creating and Deploying a SAP CAP Application with SAP HANA on SAP BTP (Cloud Foundry)
entry.content:
Note: This blog is the First Part of series - Building and Deploying SAP CAP Applications on BTP: From Database to Work Zone

 

Introduction

SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) is often demonstrated through local examples, but its real value becomes clear when the application is deployed to SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) and backed by a managed database.

In this blog, we will go through creating a basic SAP CAP application using the SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) CAP template, binding it to SAP HANA Cloud, and deploying it to SAP BTP using the Cloud Foundry runtime.

The goal here is not to build a complex application, but to establish a clean, working reference point. I start with a minimal CAP project to keep the focus on deployment rather than domain complexity.

This blog serves as a baseline for a series where the same CAP application will later be deployed using different databases and runtimes. The focus here is not application complexity, but establishing a clean, working reference point.

 

Pre-requisites:

Please make sure that you have these before starting the development:

  • SAP BTP Account
  • Cloud Foundry instance
  • Active Hana Cloud Instance
  • SAP BAS / VSCode for development
  • If using BAS, have a dev space running
  • If using VSCode then make sure that you have installed Node.js and CLI installed

 

Now, let’s start with the development.

 

Create a CAP Project from Template given in the BAS:

SAP Business Application Studio provides a template for creating a CAP project with the required structure. The positives of using the template is that it automatically builds your Multi-Tenant Application file (mta.yaml) with the required deployment modules.

 

Follow the following steps:

  • Open BAS
  • Click on the Side Menu on the top-left corner
  • Click on File
  • Click on New Project From Template
  • Select CAP Project
  • Configure the project as follows:
    • Project Name: cap-hana-app
    • Runtime: Node.js
    • Database: SAP HANA Cloud
    • Deployment Environment: Cloud Foundry
    • Runtime Capabilities: XSUAA
  • Click on Finish

 

CAP Project creation wizard with SAP HANA selected

 

This will create a CAP project for you with mta.yaml automatically updated. The generated project follows standard CAP structure:

  • app –  UI
  • db – Schema and data files
  • srv – Service Definition

 

Now that the project is created, let’s get into development.

 

Development:

Follow the following steps:

  • Define a Simple Data Model:

If you have schema.cds under db/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have schema.cds, then create one.

namespace com.mycompany.capapp;
entity Products {
    key ID          : UUID;
        name        : String(100);
        description : String(500);
        price       : Decimal(10, 2);
        stock       : Integer;
        category    : String(50);
        createdAt   : Timestamp;
        modifiedAt  : Timestamp;
}

               

  • Expose the Model via CAP Service:

If you have service.cds under srv/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have service.cds, then create one.

using com.mycompany.capapp from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
    entity Products as projection on capapp.Products;
    }

CAP automatically exposes this service as an OData endpoint without requiring manual REST configuration.

 

  • Add Data:

Run the following in the project terminal:

cds add data --records 5

 

  • Run the Application locally:

Run the following command:

cds watch

You should be able to see the data in the Products

You can also use the SQLite as a database while running locally. Execute these before starting the application:

cds build
cds deploy

 

  • Add a Managed Approuter:
  1. Right click on mta.yaml
  2. Click on Create MTA Module from Template
  3. Select Approuter Configuration
  4. Fill in the required details
  5. Click Finish

 

This is important to view the data once deployed.

 

Prepare the Project for Deployment:

Since we have created the project from the BAS Template, we already have the mta.yaml updated and deployment ready. This file already includes:

  • CAP Service Modules
  • SAP HANA HDI Container
  • Required Service Bindings

 

Build the CAP Project:

First things first, install node modules. Run the following in the Terminal:

npm i

To build the project, you can either run the following in the terminal:

mbt build

or can do it manually:

  • Right click on mta.yaml
  • Select Build MTA Project

This will build your project for deployment. Watch-out at the project structure. You might see a new addition to the structure mta_archives which consists the mtar file of the project.

 

Deploy the Application:

To deploy the application to your BTP using Cloud Foundry, you will have to login to Cloud Foundry. Run the following in Terminal:

cf login

This will ask for your subaccount credentials. Provide them and you will be logged in successfully. Remember to map the correct space and org.

Once you are logged in successfully, you are ready to deploy the application. Remember the file generated while building the CAP project, this is the same file that will be deployed.

Run the following command:

cf deploy mta_archives/cap-hana-app_1.0.0.mtar

or, you can just right click on cap-hana-app_1.0.0.mtar under mta_archives folder, and click on Deploy MTA Archive.

Successful Cloud Foundry deployment

 

During deployment:

  • HDI Container is created
  • Database schema is deployed
  • CAP Service is bound to the database automatically

Confirm the Deployed Instances

 

Verify the Deployed Application:

When the deployment is successful, you will get a application route. Copy the same and open it in the new tab of your browser and access the service endpoint: /catalog/Products

If the deployment is successful, you can see the data displayed in a JSON format.

Browser showing the OData service response

 

Conclusion

With that, we have successfully created a minimal SAP CAP application, connected it to SAP HANA Cloud, and deployed it to SAP BTP using the Cloud Foundry runtime. While the application itself is intentionally simple, the deployment pipeline we established here — from schema definition to MTA build to Cloud Foundry deployment — reflects a production-ready workflow that scales well as complexity grows.

The key takeaways from this exercise are that the BAS CAP template significantly reduces boilerplate by auto-generating the mta.yaml with the necessary modules and bindings, the HDI container handles schema deployment seamlessly, and CAP's convention-over-configuration approach means your OData service is ready without any manual REST wiring.

This baseline will serve as the foundation for the rest of this series, where we will explore deploying the same application with different databases, runtimes, and configurations — making it easier to understand the trade-offs of each approach. Stay tuned for the next part!

 

Next Steps

In the upcoming blogs, we will focus on creating the same application using a PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option.

entry.title: Joule of the century - Supercharging SAP Analytics Cloud with artificial intelligence
entry.content:
What if your analytics solution didn’t just report the numbers… but worked alongside you?

In our upcoming session, “Joule of the century – Supercharging SAP Analytics Cloud with artificial intelligence”, we’ll explore how SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP Datasphere, SAP Analytics Cloud, and Joule are reshaping planning and analytics with embedded AI.

The thing is, we’re all navigating the same challenge: dashboards everywhere, insights nowhere.  AI changes that.  Generate KPIs in seconds.  Turn plain language into analysis.  Create formulas and SQL without the blank-page anxiety.  Even deploy autonomous AI agents that reason, plan, and act.

And yes, we’ll show how Joule moves from “Tell me how” to “Do this task” to “Give me insights” seamlessly inside your workflows.

If you’re working in xP&A and wondering how to bring real AI value to customers, this is the session.

Register now and see what happens when analytics finally feels… effortless.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/feb/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00013148.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPAnalyticsCloud #SAPDatasphere #BusinessAI #xPA

entry.title: Entitlements
entry.content:
Introduction : 

In the SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP), Entitlements play a crucial role in defining how services and resources are allocated across different environments. They act as the foundation for enabling platform capabilities, ensuring that organizations can control usage based on purchased or subscribed service plans.

Understanding Entitlements becomes even more important when managing large landscapes that include multiple Global Accounts, Directories, and Subaccounts. This is where Entity Assignments and Service Assignments come into the picture. Entity Assignments allow administrators to distribute entitled service plans to specific subaccounts or directories, while Service Assignments focus on enabling and configuring those services within a given subaccount.

In this blog, we will explore the concept of Entitlements in SAP BTP and take a closer look at how Entity Assignments and Service Assignments work together to ensure proper service availability, governance, and resource management across the platform.




1 What is  Entitlements ?          

Entitlements in SAP BTP are the authorized usage of specific SAP BTP services and service plans within a global account

2 Types of Entitlements in SAP BTP ?

1 Entity Assignment

2 Service Assignment

 1 Entity Assignment ?

Entity Assignment is the process of assigning entitlements from the Global Account to a Subaccount.

Why Entity Assignment is Needed?

Because:

  • Entitlements are owned by Global Account
  • Applications are run inside Subaccounts
  • So you must assign first, then use.

 2 Service Assignment?

Service Assignment is assigning a specific service plan to a Subaccount or Space so developers can create instances.

How to Navigate to Entity Assignment

  • First we need to login BTP Cockpit
  • After click on Go To Your Trial Account shown in below.
    • After we can see Entitlements here

      => After we need to click on Entity Assignments

           Here we need to mention our Subaccounts/Directories:

      And here we can see services

      => After we will click on Edit Button
      we can see Add Service Plans Button here

      • Here Click on Add Service Plans Button

      After we can see Add service Plans  here select one service and click on Add service Plans Button

      • After in search bar search which service you need to add

                 This is all about Entity Assignment

        Service Assignment:

        When you click on Service Assignment we can see all available service here


        Conclusion : 

        Entitlements form the backbone of service management in SAP BTP by controlling which services and plans are available within an organization’s landscape. Through Entity Assignments, administrators can strategically distribute these entitlements to directories and subaccounts based on project, team, or business requirements. Service Assignments then take this a step further by enabling the actual consumption and configuration of services within specific subaccounts.

        By clearly understanding the relationship between Entitlements, Entity Assignments, and Service Assignments, organizations can ensure optimal resource utilization, maintain governance, and avoid unnecessary service limitations. Proper entitlement management not only improves operational efficiency but also supports scalable and secure cloud adoption within SAP BTP environments.

entry.title: Implementing B2B Advisory in SAP Integration Suite for Custom XML EDIFACT Scenario Part - 2
entry.content:
In Part 1, I explained the overall setup of B2B Advisory. How the TPM foundation was structured. With the Company, Trading Partner, and Systems configuration in place, the next critical layer is the Agreement.

In B2B Advisory, the Agreement is not just a configuration object — it is the runtime decision engine. Every inbound or outbound message must resolve to a valid Agreement. If it does not, the processing stops at the monitoring layer.

In this part, I will focus on how Agreement determination works how it can be create, how it interacts with payload identifiers, and what challenges arise when dealing with XML-based EDIFACT.

What is Agreement

In simple language, an Agreement in B2B Advisory defines the rules under which a message is allowed to be processed between two parties. It answers four basic questions:

  • Who is sending the message?
  • Who is receiving the message?
  • What type of message is it?
  • In which direction is it flowing (Inbound or Outbound)?

If all these conditions match, the system allows the message to proceed. If not, processing stops.

You can think of an Agreement as a contract between two systems. Unless a valid contract exists, the message cannot move forward.

Agreement Templates

Agreement template are created for the refernce of an actual agreement. It consist a desgin flow of how actual end-2-end B2B flow works. Who it the party (Our business or Trading partner), what all systems are involve, communication adapters information what messgae are we using, invlolved identifer. This is just a template where all the input values can be changed later on while creating an actual agreement.

Agreement

This is the core of the B2B Advisory.  While creating an agreement, you can take refernce of an existing agreement template so that a desing flow with the connected systems wil automatically populated.

Working with copy with template --> agreement will copy the existing configuration available in selected template and give the option to modify as requried. (preferable choice)

Working with bind with template --> agreement will bind with the existing configruation and doesnot allow to change configuration. 

Agreement Overview

This include the overview of your agreement, who is the initiator, connected type system, SAP system and what are the identifers involve to check wether the incoming messgae have one of these identifer. These identifer are not entered manually instead is take refernce from the profile (Company/Trading profile). You have a option to create on prompt but advisable is to take refernce from the profile (Single/Group Identifer).

Learning from agreement
  • While creating an agreement sometime a confusion arise that, for one trading partner if we have multiple types of business transactions, then should we create multiple agreements. No, we have options to create multiple business transaction scenario in one agreement (inbound and outbound both). Don't treat an agreement as one interface, agreement is something a contract between your orgnization and trading partner. So while create an agreement name it as per the involve trading partner.
  • For custom formats there is not option I have found for the Custom type system, So I decided to use type system as "SAP S/4HANA On Premise IDoc" for Trading Partner (considered after various failed hit and try). As based on the Type system standard iFlow behaves differntly and most suitable to work with the XML "SAP S/4HANA On Premise IDoc" is best as per my R&D.
  • We have options to create custom MIG & MAG under custom type system but there is no options I found to include those in the agreement. So I Have used standard MIG of each messgae for my Integration.
  • While fetching the standard MIGs message version are available but there is no guarantee the SAP version you have will be available. In my case for ORDERS.ORDER05 have two versions "S4HANA 1709–Version - S4HANA 1709" and "1809_FPS02–Version - 1809_FPS02" and for DESADV.DELVRY07 I see only "1809_FPS02–Version - 1809_FPS02". Where as in our landscape we have "S/4HANA 2023". 
  • Key consideration is choosing different version on the IDoc may create issue, as the metadata available in yours SAP will not be the same available in the MIG you selected. So instead of choosing MIG in you mapping design, we refer the structure what is available in the SAP. Also, MIG selection is mandatory in the agreement and agreement is based on the Type System, so ultimatly in the Type system (Company/Trading partner profile') mentioned version what you have in you SAP and what is available in you MIG.
    S/4HANA Version (MIG)ABAP/Basis Release (IDoc)
    1709752
    1809753
    1909754
    2020755
    2023758

 
B2B Scenario

Next step will be creating the transactions all inbounds and outbounds with the selected trading partner, the actual business flow.

Here we have the designed flow which have the prebuild steps as:

Sender & Receiver can be your SAP system or Trading partner system depends on the what you have choose on the transaction create screen where Interchange have the message and involve type system version information (version/MIG can be editied manually).

Other features what we have here are the Activity Paramters, Custom Search Attributes and Custom Rules. 

Activity Parameter -->These can be used for additional mapping logic. for example storing partner profile code, logical system deatils, port details, DUNS number, EDI messgae version, other hardcoded values per trading partner. These can be manually create or can be extend from Company/Trading parter profile. In short partner specific values can be maintained here.

Custom Search Value --> To get more filters for the monitoring such as any messgae id, plant, ship-to, buyer-id etc. These can be added with the help of parameters, headers and XPath.

Transaction Configuration

After these configuration we have to start mantain the channels, mapping, MIG version etc. In the Source Communication we need to maintain the channle, these are not actual channle/adapter instead a template which we create in Company/Trading profile. Actual adapter which will pick the file, need to develop in cloud integration (IFlow designer) which will execute these agreement transaction.

In the inerchnage mentioned the actual MIG used, Number range object, and the version of your SAP system. There are certain other options also we have in case those are requrired as per your requirement. As in my case I have the customized XML of EDIFact file I have used the MIG which is available for me for the business process I have and in the SAP Version I have chaged from original MIG to the one which we have in our landscape. I have used the same process for both sender/receiver interchange.

Mapping Logic --> We have the option to execute the mapping developed in Cloud Integration via process direct or we can use the MAG whatever suite the requirement. In my case because of custom logics I am haved process direct which is calling a seperate iflow developed in cloud Integation.

Once everythig is in there place after activating the agreement, all the information present till agreement will be executed in the partner directory data, which is the runtime enviournment for execution.

Here the Activity Plain ID the the matching string, once that is found correct agreement will be executed else the that transaction will fail with the error "Agreement Configuration Access Failed". 

What This Plan ID Template Represents --> The Agreement Plan ID is constructed by combining multiple message and partner attributes into a single structured string.

  • sndAdapterType --> The communication adapter (Process_Direct, AS2, IDoc, etc.)
  • sndDocumentStandard --> EDIFACT, X12, SAP_IDoc etc.
  • sndMessageNamespace --> Namespace maintained in design
  • sndMessageVersion -->  Version like D96A, 758 etc.
  • dynamic internal placeholder --> %s
  • sndSenderIdQualifier -->  Sender ID Identifier (single/group)
  • sndSenderPartnerType -->  Type of partner (Customer, Vendor)
  • sndSenderSystemId -->  Sender system identifier
  • sndSenderSystemIdQualifier -->  Qualifier for sender system
  • dynamic internal placeholder --> %s
  • sndReceiverIdQualifier -->  Receiver qualifier
  • sndReceiverPartnerType -->  Receiver partner type
  • sndReceiverSystemId -->  Receiver system
  • sndReceiverSystemIdQualifier -->  Receiver qualifier
  • sndMessageType -->  Message type like ORDERS
sndAdapterType + "-" + sndDocumentStandard + "-" + sndMessageNamespace + "-" + sndMessageVersion + "-%s-" + sndSenderIdQualifier + "-" + sndSenderPartnerType + "-" + sndSenderSystemId + "-" + sndSenderSystemIdQualifier + "-%s-" + sndReceiverIdQualifier + "-" + sndReceiverPartnerType + "-" + sndReceiverSystemId + "-" + sndReceiverSystemIdQualifier + "-" + sndMessageType

This is how an end-2-end configuration of agreement works. In the next step I will show how standard package behaves with this agreement and what changes I made in one of the flow for inbound custom XML based EDIFact.

link (will be update once published) : Implementing B2B Advisory in SAP Integration Suite for Custom XML EDIFACT Scenarios part - 3

 

Thanks

Aman Sharma

entry.title: SAP Developer News, February 26th, 2026
entry.content:
ITEMS

March Developer Challenge Announcement - ABAP 

  • Application Development and Automation Blog Posts on the SAP Community https://community.sap.com/t5/application-development-and-automation-blog-posts/bg-p/application-developmentblog-board
  • Learn about the SAP Joule for Developers, ABAP AI Capabilities https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-development-tools-user-guide/joule-for-developers-abap-ai-capabilities-f14ebffef77b41bfb0746c33dcb70e84 

Implementing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) in an SAP Environment 

  • Implementing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) in an SAP Environment: https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-architecture-knowledge-base/implementing-retrieval-augmented-generation-rag-in-an-sap-environment/ta-p/14325588  
  • SAP CodeJam Topics: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-codejam-blog-posts/sap-codejam-topics/ba-p/221407#codejam1  
  • SAP CodeJam Roadshow 2026 - Brazil edition: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-codejam-blog-posts/sap-codejam-roadshow-2026-brazil-edition-kicking-off-in-march-and/ba-p/14317926

SAP BTP ABAP Environment - Release 2602 

  • Blog post https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-btp-abap-environment-release-2602/ba-p/14329182 

Integration Suite updates 

  • What's new in SAP Cloud Integration https://help.sap.com/whats-new/cf0cb2cb149647329b5d02aa96303f56?locale=en-US&lastSent=2026-02-16&Component=SAP+Cloud+Application+Event+Hub;Integration%2BSuite%2BGeneral%2BFeatures;Event%2BMesh;Edge%2BIntegration%2BCell;Cloud%2BIntegration;API%2BManagement&Valid_as_Of=2026-01-01:2026-03-31  

SAP Audit Log Viewer for Global Accounts and SAP Audit Log Viewer Now Automatically Enabled for All Subaccounts 

  • SAP Audit Log Viewer service for the Cloud Foundry Environment https://help.sap.com/docs/btp/sap-business-technology-platform/audit-log-viewer-for-cloud-foundry-environment  

DYK #5 - Search ABAP CDS Objects using Annotations 

  • ABAP Development Tools for Eclipse guide https://help.sap.com/docs/abap-cloud/abap-development-tools-user-guide/about-abap-development-tools-user-guide  

CHAPTER TITLES

00:00 Intro 
00:07 March Developer Challenge Announcement-ABAP 
01:25 Implementing Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) in an SAP Environment 
03:23 SAP BTP ABAP Environment - Release 2602 
04:42 Integration Suite updates 
05:35 SAP Audit Log Viewer for Global Accounts and SAP Audit Log Viewer Now Automatically Enabled for All Subaccounts 
06:30 DYK #5 - Search ABAP CDS Objects using Annotations 

TRANSCRIPT

This is the SAP Developer News for February 26th, 2026.

[Mamikee]
Hey everyone, we're kicking off this year's SAP Developer Challenge with a topic that's very close to home, ABAP. With AI dominating conversations across the tech world, we thought this month would be the perfect time for our community to explore Joule for Developers. SAP Joule for Developers brings embedded AI capabilities to SAP Build and ABAP, helping you accelerate app and extension development, reduce development costs, and boost overall developer productivity. Now, for this challenge, we'll be using the basic trial offering for SAP Build, which includes an SAP BTP ABAP environment with Joule for Developers. Here's how it will work. So in week one, we'll focus on setting up your ABAP instance from the basic trial in ADT and start exploring some of Joule' key capabilities as the weeks go on. The challenge will run for four weeks starting Monday, March 2nd and ending March 23rd. But don't worry, we'll keep submissions open until the end of the month for anyone running a little behind. Now, to count toward the overall monthly challenge, make sure you complete and submit each week's task. Week 1 drops on the SAP Community on Monday, March 2nd, so get ready, and we can't wait to see what you build.

[Witalij]
At the recent SAP Now AI event I attended, most of our customers showcased their AI implementations using the Retrieval Augmented Generation, or RAG, approach. RAG grounds generative AI applications in private business data, delivering accurate and trustworthy responses. SAP Business Technology Platform supports three RAG patterns, as outlined by my friend Lyuba Nakryyko from the BTP Center of Excellence. First, custom RAG with SAP HANA Cloud Vector Engine. Best suited for structured text, such as product descriptions or service notes. This method converts data into semantic embeddings within SAP HANA Cloud, enabling context-aware responses from large language models. Second, Knowledge Graph Engine in SAP HANA Cloud is optimized for structured data with complex relationships, such as supply chains or bills of materials. It maps entities and their relationships, translates queries into precise data retrieval operations, and feeds relevant facts to the language model. Third, Managed Document Grounding, a turnkey solution for document-based Q&A, for example on PDFs, in SAP AI Core. It automates document processing and retrieval, integrating smoothly with SAP Joule or Generative AI Hub for rapid deployment. Each pattern caters to different data types and structured text, structured relationships, or document repositories, enabling you to choose the approach that best fits your business and technical needs. Lyuba's article on SAP Community is linked in the description. Also in the description, you will find a link to SAP CodeJam topics, which explore hands-on opportunities covering these different approaches in SAP Business Technology Platform. The closest SAP CodeJam events will be held in Brazil in April, and sign -ups are already open. Sim, amigos. Estamos a caminho. Até logo.

[Rich]
Hey, folks. Rich Heilman here. Long time. No see. I had to bribe Sheena and Shilpa this week to allow me to do the news piece, so here goes. That's right, folks, it's that time again. SAP BTP ABAP Environment 2602 has been released with more ABAP goodness for all of our ABAP developers out there. Nora Klemp from ABAP Product Management has posted a blog post outlining all of the new features. Some notable new features include Collaborative Draft, which allows developers to create applications which leverage draft capabilities across business objects. Recommendations is another cool feature that enhances the dropdown list or value helps with recommendations on what the value could or should be based on context and some AI magic. Lots of other new features as well, including a new My Home Page as the landing page for ABAP systems, which will have additional features added in over time. For more information on all this ABAP good stuff, please check out the link to Nora's blog post and get all the details today. See you next time, friends. Bye for now.

[Antonio]
Hola, SAP Developers. As usual, many things happening in the integration space. And I just want to share with you some of the highlights in case you missed them. In Cloud Integration, there are many new adapters. Microsoft Outlook, Zendesk Receiver Adapter, Bamboo HR Receiver Adapter. There's also a new version of the Shopify Adapter that supports OAuth 2 client credentials. Related to Groovy Scripts, you can now upload Groovy Script 1.x and 2.0 in the Script Collection artifact and in the integration flow. When you optimize Groovy scripts with AI, you can now get a review report and download the original script. In API Management, we can now discover and publish events from Advanced Event Mesh in Developer Hub. I'm including the relevant links in the description, so make sure to check them out. Hasta luego!

[DJ]
It's always worth keeping an eye on the What's New for SAP Business Technology Platform facility on the SAP Help Portal for upcoming features and releases. Something that will likely be of particular interest for us all is the very recent general availability of the SAP Audit Log Viewer for SAP BTP in the cockpit. It's available for audit logs at the global account, so you can look at entitlements and assignments made, for example, And also, you can look at the creation and deletion of subaccounts. And talking of subaccounts, it's also available for all subaccounts too. You don't need any entitlement or subscription to the corresponding service to use it. Check out the documentation. Links in the description.

[Shilpa]
Did you know you can search the CDS View entity by the annotations used? Choose the annotation. Now, this is the annotation indicating extensibility enabled. Choose Get Various List from the context. Choose the required object types, apply filter to restrict the search. Use control space to get the options. And click Finish. The Various List is opened in the search view and displays the search result as a list. Similarly, you can also choose the value of annotation and repeat the same. Here I have selected the authorization check annotation with value as check. So these are the CDS views which has the authorization marked as required. Not just the annotation, you can also search the usage of view entity in the similar way. Choose the view entity and select get where used list. Now this result gives the list of CDS view entity where it has been used.

entry.title: LDAP Based User Management in SAP Cloud Connector
entry.content:
In this blog I will explain about how we can use LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) to manage Cloud Connector users and authentication.

After installation of the Cloud Connector, it uses file-based user management by default.

With file-base user management we can login with Administrator user.

Alternatively, the Cloud Connector also supports LDAP based user management.

With LDAP based user management, users are authenticated against your company’s LDAP directory service. We can check from the below screen as user store shows LDAP.

Advantage of using LDAP based user management:

LDAP based user management can authenticate the users based on provided roles. These roles can be used to differentiate between users who are only allowed to view the configuration and those who are responsible for the administration functions.

Below are the steps to activate LDAP for user administration in SAP Cloud Connector:

We need to connect with our LDAP team to get the below details:

  •  LDAP Server name and port – Default port is 389, but if we want to use secure connection then port 636 is being used. Make sure ports are open from cloud connector server to LDAP server.
  • Root and Intermediate certificates of LDAP server– This need to be imported in Cloud Connector side.
  • SCC user account details that can login to LDAP server.
  • LDAP group sccadmin for SAP Cloud Connector - A role is a group in LDAP terms. The Cloud Connector provides the following roles: sccadmin, sccsupport, sccmonitoring and sccdisplay.

Once we get the above details we can start as per below steps:

Step 1: Login to SAP Cloud Connector with Administrator user and navigate to Configuration. Under User Interface tab select the Switch to LDAP button as mentioned in below screenshot:

Step 2: Provide the LDAP server's host and port (port 389 is used by default) in the <Host> field. To use the secure protocol variant LDAPS based on TLS, select Secure and user port 636.

Step 3: Provide a service user and its password in the fields Connection Username and Connection Password.

Step 4: In the Search Rules configuration, the most important point is to mention the correct syntax and attribute based on your LDAP configuration settings. Below is the example which I used in my scenario.

  • roleBase: groups information we can get it from LDAP team, where they have created the roles.
  • userbase: users information we can get it from LDAP team, where they have created the users or where the users are located.
  • userRoleName="memberOf”: This information we can get it from LDAP team, which syntax is for user role name.
  • userSearch="(sAMAccountName={0})”: This information we can get it from LDAP team, which syntax is for user search. This is the preferred attribute often used for Active Directory, sAMAccountName, the corresponding Search Rules configuration is userSearch="(sAMAccountName={0})". For example, userSearch="(uid={0})" can also be used if uid attribute is being used in LDAP.
  • roleSubtree="true": group nodes on the LDAP server may be located under several branches inside the "base" branch. In this case, add the boolean attribute roleSubtree="true".
  • userSubtree="true": user nodes on the LDAP server may be located under several branches inside the "base" branch. In this case, add the boolean attribute roleSubtree="true".

Step 5: Below are the available roles that can be assigned to users based on the requirement:

  • sccadmin: This role has full administrative rights in the cloud connector.
  • sccdisplay: This role enables the display of all data in the cloud connector.
  • sccsupport: This role is used to display data and support activities (e.g., to activate traces).
  • sccmonitoring: This role is required to access the monitoring APIs of the cloud connector.

Below is the example for setting up custom roles (sccadmin) in cloud connector

Step 6: Set up the LDAP Trust Store. When we are configuring LDAP for User, the LDAP Trust Store can be set up by adding certificates to the Allowlist and choosing a Trust Option.

There are three trust options:

  • Trust all: All certificates are trusted. This is the least secure option which does not consider any trusted certificates, in the allowlist or otherwise, and simply trusts all certificates.
  • Use LDAP trust store only: Only the certificates listed in the allowlist are trusted.
  • Use JDK and LDAP trust store: The certificates in the allowlist and those of the JDK are trusted.

An LDAP connection over TLS can cause TLS errors if the LDAP server uses a certificate that is not signed by a trusted CA. We can use the keytool utility for importing the certificates which we have received from LDAP team. Usually, the trust storage location is cacerts inside the java directory (jre/lib/security/cacerts).

Below is the command for importing the certificate:

"C:\SAP\sapjvm-8.1.107\sapjvm_8\jre\bin\keytool.exe" -import -trustcacerts -file "D:\Cert_Chain\certificate.cer" -alias certificate -keystore "C:\SAP\sapjvm-8.1.107\sapjvm_8\jre\lib\security\cacerts" -storepass changeit

Step 7: Once all the above steps are completed save the configuration as draft and test the configuration by using the below button:

Step 8: Once test LDAP configuration is successful, Activate the configuration. This will restart the cloud connector.

Step 9: After the restart of cloud connector, we can login to cloud connector with user as per company’s LDAP directory service.

Note:

In case of any issue in Test LDAP configuration, kindly check with LDAP team for the correct syntax and attribute for userbase and rolebase. You can also install ldp on the cloud connector server and check the connectivity and binding with the service users and find out the correct attributes.

entry.title: The Hidden Cost of Custom Code in SAP IS U — And How BTP Reduces It Without a Big Bang
entry.content:
Executive Summary

For years, SAP IS‑U has quietly carried the weight of utility operations. Billing accuracy, regulatory compliance, and meter‑to‑cash stability always came first — and rightly so. But the side effect of that stability is a landscape full of Z‑programs, user exits, and enhancements that were built for good reasons at the time, yet now create friction when utilities try to modernize.

As the industry shifts toward decarbonization, DER growth, and real‑time customer expectations, the question isn’t “Is custom code a problem?” It’s “How do we reduce its impact without breaking the systems that keep the lights on?”

SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) offers a practical, low‑risk way to separate custom logic from the core and prepare for S/4HANA — without a disruptive rewrite or a multi‑year freeze.

1. The Custom Code Reality Inside SAP IS‑U

 Most custom code in IS‑U wasn’t a mistake. It was a necessity.

Utilities added enhancements to:

  • Meet state‑specific regulatory rules
  • Support complex rate structures
  • Integrate with legacy CIS, AMI, and market systems
  • Automate workflows long before APIs or event frameworks existed

Over time, this created:

  • Thousands of Z‑objects
  • Enhancements buried deep inside billing, device management, and FICA
  • Business logic tightly coupled to SAP’s upgrade cycle

What once delivered flexibility now slows down change.

2. The Hidden Costs Most Programs Underestimate

2.1 Upgrade and Migration Friction

Every enhancement inside the core increases:

  • SP and EHP regression testing effort
  • S/4HANA conversion complexity
  • Dependency on niche IS‑U technical expertise

Custom code does not just slow upgrades — it dictates upgrade timing.

2.2 Operational Risk

Many utilities still rely on:

  • Poorly documented enhancements
  • Knowledge concentrated in a few long‑tenured developers
  • Custom logic triggered deep inside critical transactions

This creates silent operational risk — especially during incident response or regulatory audits.

2.3 Innovation Tax

Each new digital initiative must answer an uncomfortable question:

“Do we modify the core again, or build around it?”

When the core is overloaded with custom logic, innovation slows — not because ideas are weak, but because change becomes expensive and risky.

3. Why ‘Rewrite Everything’ Is Not a Strategy

Most utilities cannot absorb:

  • A full custom code rewrite
  • A large‑scale CIS replacement
  • A multi‑year freeze on business change

A big‑bang approach often introduces more risk than it removes.

A more realistic path is selective decoupling — and that’s where BTP fits naturally.

4. How SAP BTP Reduces Custom Code Impact — Pragmatically

BTP gives utilities a way to shift custom logic out of the core while keeping IS‑U stable.

4.1 Keep the Core Clean — for Real

Instead of embedding logic in IS‑U:

  • Business rules move to BTP services
  • Extensions run side‑by‑side, not inside ECC or S/4HANA
  • Core processes remain upgrade‑friendly

4.2 Event‑Driven Extensions

Using events and APIs:

  • IS‑U triggers events (billing completion, device updates, move‑in/out)
  • BTP processes logic asynchronously
  • Failures are isolated, not system‑wide

4.3 Reusable Innovation Layer

Once on BTP, the same extension framework can support:

  • DER orchestration
  • AI‑based billing analytics
  • Customer self‑service enhancements
  • Regulatory reporting automation

This turns custom logic into assets, not liabilities.

5. Reference Architecture (Conceptual)

Below is a simplified conceptual architecture illustrating how SAP IS‑U and BTP coexist.

This model allows utilities to:

  • Retire high‑risk custom code gradually
  • Introduce new capabilities without touching the core
  • Prepare for S/4HANA on their own timeline

6. A Phased, Utility‑Friendly Adoption Model

Phase 1: Observe

  • Identify custom code hotspots
  • Monitor usage, performance, and change frequency

Phase 2: Isolate

  • Move non‑core logic to BTP extensions
  • Replace enhancements with APIs and events

Phase 3: Optimize

  • Retire unused Z‑objects
  • Standardize extension patterns
  • Accelerate future innovation

This approach delivers value within existing budgets and without destabilizing operations. 

7. Final Thought for Utility Leaders

The goal is not to eliminate custom code overnight. The goal is to stop letting yesterday’s decisions dictate tomorrow’s options.

SAP BTP provides a practical, low‑risk path to reduce technical debt, lower long‑term cost, and modernize SAP IS‑U — without a big bang and without compromising the core.

If you are evaluating S/4HANA readiness, IS‑U modernization, or clean‑core strategies, the conversation should start with architecture, not migration dates.

If you are evaluating S/4HANA readiness, IS‑U modernization, or clean‑core strategies, the conversation should start with architecture — not migration dates.

entry.title: Mass Invoice Reversals Without Customer Panic: A Clean Core, BTP Driven Strategy for ModernUtilities
entry.content:
Mass Invoice Reversals Without Customer Panic: A Clean‑Core, BTP‑Driven Strategy for Modern Utilities

Executive Summary

If you’ve spent any time around utility billing operations, you know mass reversals aren’t rare — they’re part of the job. Sometimes it’s a rate issue, sometimes a meter read problem, and sometimes it’s just one of those unexpected data surprises that ripple across months of billing, these reversals often span multiple billing cycles and thousands of customers. While the operational process may take days to complete, the customer impact is immediate: as soon as reversals post, customers see large credits on their online portals. This triggers confusion, social media chatter, call‑center spikes, and reputational risk before corrected bills are ready.

What I want to share here is a practical way utilities can handle this without rewriting IS‑U or scrambling teams at 6 AM. We used SAP BTP to create a small visibility layer that sits between IS‑U and the customer portal — and honestly, it solved a problem that has bothered billing teams for years, event‑driven architecture, and lightweight AI to prevent premature credit visibility without modifying SAP IS‑U or customer‑facing systems. The solution introduces a “Billing Visibility Orchestration Layer” that controls what customers see during mass reversals, ensuring operational accuracy without customer panic.

The Practitioner Reality: When a Simple Reversal Becomes a Public Crisis

Every utility billing team has lived through this scenario:

  • A mass correction requires reversing 8–12 months of invoices.
  • The reversal job runs for hours or days.
  • As soon as reversals post, customers see large credits on the portal.
  • Screenshots appear on social media: “Utility X just gave me $900 credit!”
  • Call centers are overwhelmed.
  • Regulators ask questions.
  • The billing team is still in the middle of the correction.

The root cause is simple: SAP IS‑U posts reversals immediately, and customer portals read them immediately. And here’s the part nobody talks about: IS‑U has not built‑in buffer. The moment a reversal posts, the customer sees it. There’s no ‘hold this until we’re done’ button. That’s where the trouble starts. Utilities cannot legally notify customers in advance, and they cannot delay the operational correction. This creates a structural gap between internal billing processes and external customer visibility.

Why This Happens: A Technical Gap in Traditional IS‑U

SAP IS‑U was designed with a real‑time posting model:

  • Reversal document posts →
  • FICA updates →
  • Portal reads →
  • Customer sees credit

There is no concept of:

  • “Temporary suppression”
  • “Billing in progress”
  • “Visibility hold”
  • “Batch publish”

This is not a failure of IS‑U — it’s simply a design that predates modern digital customer expectations.

The Modern Solution: A Billing Visibility Orchestration Layer on SAP BTP

Instead of modifying IS‑U or customer portals, utilities can introduce a lightweight, clean‑core extension on SAP BTP that controls visibility, not billing.

This layer sits between:

SAP IS‑U → BTP → Customer Portal / CIS / Mobile App

It does not change billing logic. It does not alter FICA. It does not modify the portal. It simply orchestrates when billing information becomes visible.

Key Capabilities

  • Detect mass reversals in real time
  • Flag accounts as “under correction”
  • Temporarily suppress credit visibility
  • Replace credit display with a neutral message
  • Release final billing once corrections are complete

This simple layer kept customers from seeing half‑baked credits and gave the billing team the breathing room they needed to finish the job properly.

Textual Representation

 

How It Works Using BTP Services

1. SAP Event Mesh — Detect Reversals in Real Time

Event Mesh listens for:

  • Billing document reversals
  • FKK document reversals
  • Mass job triggers

When reversal volume exceeds a threshold (e.g., >500 accounts), it triggers the suppression workflow.

2. SAP BTP Workflow — Manage the Correction Window

Workflow orchestrates:

  • Start of reversal window
  • Suppression period
  • Completion of rebilling
  • Release of final invoice

This creates a controlled, auditable process.

3. SAP BTP Business Rules — Define Visibility Logic

Rules can be configured without coding:

  • “If reversal count > X, suppress visibility for Y hours.”
  • “If account is in correction, show neutral message.”
  • “If rebill fails, extend suppression.”

This keeps the solution clean‑core and upgrade‑safe.

4. SAP HANA Cloud — Temporary Data Store

Stores:

  • Accounts under correction
  • Suppression flags
  • Workflow status
  • Rebill completion indicators

No changes to IS‑U tables.

5. SAP BTP AI — Predict and Prevent Customer Impact

AI can:

  • Identify accounts likely to call
  • Detect social media spikes
  • Predict rebill duration
  • Recommend suppression windows
  • Flag anomalies in reversal patterns

This turns a reactive process into a proactive one.

What the Customer Sees Instead

Instead of a large credit, the portal displays:

“Your billing is being updated. Your final balance will be available shortly.”

This avoids:

  • Panic
  • Misinterpretation
  • Social media escalation
  • Call center overload

Clean‑Core Alignment

This approach is fully clean‑core because:

  • No IS‑U modifications
  • No FICA changes
  • No portal rewrites
  • No custom Z‑tables in SAP
  • All logic runs on BTP
  • Fully upgrade‑safe
  • Works with ECC and S/4HANA

This is exactly what SAP means by “extensibility without modification.”

Practitioner Example

In one of the utilities I worked with, we had to reverse nearly 10 months of bills for over 180,000 customers. Within 45 minutes, screenshots of huge credits were all over social media. The call center was drowning. Regulators were pinging leadership. It was chaos — and all because the portal updated too early.

After implementing a BTP‑based visibility layer:

  • Mass reversals ran silently
  • No customer saw temporary credits
  • No social media chatter
  • No call center spike
  • Corrected bills were published cleanly

The billing team completed the same process with zero customer disruption.

Conclusion

Mass invoice reversals will always be part of utility operations. But customer visibility does not need to be immediate or uncontrolled. By introducing a Billing Visibility Orchestration Layer on SAP BTP — powered by Event Mesh, Workflow, Business Rules, HANA Cloud, and AI — utilities can protect customer experience, reduce operational risk, and maintain clean‑core principles.

This is modernization where it matters: not in rewriting billing logic, but in orchestrating how and when customers see the results.

entry.title: ABAP Cloud Learning Map
entry.content:
The ABAP Cloud development model (ABAP Cloud) is the foundation of ABAP-based development at SAP, its partners, and customers, designed from the ground up to adhere to clean core principles. It brings together evolving technologies and methodologies into a sustainable, long-lasting programming model. 

The ABAP Cloud development model encompasses all aspects of modern cloud-ready software development, resulting in a wide range of topics that need to be understood. Therefore, clear guidance is helpful for getting started with and navigating ABAP Cloud.

Discover the ABAP Cloud Learning Map🗺️ – your guide to mastering the ABAP Cloud development model. It provides an overview of essential topics and skills, along with a curated collection of links to key resources covering the technologies, tools, approaches, and practices that drive cloud-ready, upgrade-stable ABAP development. 📚Whether you’re starting out or filling knowledge gaps, the learning map helps you navigate modern ABAP Cloud development at your own pace. 

🔗Access it here: ABAP Cloud Learning Map↗

entry.title: Automating SAP HANA Cloud (Other Environment) DB Lifecycle Management with SAP Automation Pilot
entry.content:
In our recent updates around AI-enabled Ops Operations, we demonstrated how SAP Automation Pilot boosts structured, scalable, and intelligent automation for Day-2 Ops Agents through Joule.

Today, I am excited to take this one step further.

🚀  We are expanding our automation coverage to fully address SAP HANA Cloud lifecycle management in the Other Environment - bringing operational excellence to database management at scale.

From Standard Catalogs to Broader Lifecycle Automation

SAP Automation Pilot already provides a built-in catalog for managing SAP HANA Cloud database instances running in the Cloud Foundry environment - that is the the well-known SAP BTP Database Lifecycle Management (dblm-sapcp) Catalog.

This catalog has successfully helped customers automate essential lifecycle tasks directly within SAP BTP.

However, many customers also operate SAP HANA Cloud instances in the Other Environment, where lifecycle management is equally critical and operationally sensitive. While SAP Automation Pilot already offers all the technical capabilities required to automate those tasks, we recognized the need to package common scenarios into a structured, reusable, and publicly available catalog.

That is exactly what we have now delivered! 

Introducing: SAP HANA Cloud Lifecycle Management Sample Catalog (Other Environment)

We have consolidated a wide range of SAP HANA Cloud lifecycle scenarios into a dedicated public sample catalog, now available in our GitHub repository here: SAP HANA Cloud Lifecycle Management Sample Catalog (Other Environment)

This catalog provides a comprehensive and validated set of automation commands for managing SAP HANA Cloud instances at subaccount level using Service Manager.

Instead of executing repetitive and sensitive database operations manually, you can now automate domains such as: 🔹 Instance Lifecycle Management 🔹Backup & Recovery 🔹 Snapshot Management 🔹 Instance Configuration🔹 Upgrades & Plugins 🔹 Elastic Compute Nodes (ECN), etc. 

✅ All commands are validated and ready to be explored by our users - to help you minimize manual effort, reduce risk, and standardize operational procedures.

Beyond Automation: Toward Intelligent Database Operations

While structured lifecycle automation already delivers immediate value, the real transformation begins when automation becomes agentic.

Because all HANA Cloud-related commands are exposed through SAP Automation Pilot, they can be orchestrated, combined, and consumed in higher-level operational workflows. 

This naturally leads to the next evolution - AI Ops Agents for HANA Cloud enabled with SAP Automation Pilot and Joule! 

📖Be Part of the Journey - Join Our Customer Engagement Initiative

As we continue expanding agentic AI operations, collaboration with customers remains key.

If you would like to gain hands-on experience with AI-enabled Ops automation and help shape the future of intelligent database operations, we invite you to subscribe in our ongoing Customer Engagement Initiative: Agentic AI Operations with SAP Automation Pilot

Through this initiative, you can:

  • Explore real-world AI-driven automation scenarios;
  • Bring your own use cases and operational challenges;
  • Provide direct feedback to the product team;
  • Influence upcoming capabilities;

⏳ Registration is open until March 15, 2026 - secure your spot soon!

From structured lifecycle automation to fully agentic database operations - this is the next chapter of operational excellence with SAP Automation Pilot.

Let’s build it together!

entry.title: Customize SAPUI5 apps for business users – make an impact by joining our CEI project
entry.content:
SAP Customer Engagement Initiatives (CEI) on https://influence.sap.com allow you, as an SAP customer or partner, to gain early insights into SAP's product developments and work directly with SAP teams to shape future product directions. This project presents a unique opportunity to work with SAPUI5 flexibility product managers and engineering teams, leveraging your expertise customizing SAPUI5 apps with key user adaptation.

Join the upcoming CEI project to make an impact on how key users interact with key user adaptation

If you’re already convinced – join here. If not, read on 🙂

What is key user adaptation?

Key user adaptation is a mode that allows extending SAPUI5 applications for other users right in the browser. It is highly intuitive and requires no technical or programming skills. Just use drag and drop to make UI changes – no modification or copy of SAP standard coding necessary! Read more here.

What is the targeted improvement?

Although the product is mature and widely used, we actively evolve it by delivering new functionality and capability enhancements alongside continuous user experience improvements, informed by UI testing and direct customer feedback. 

We are in the process of overhauling key user adaptation’s UI, simplifying the overall approach while making often-used scenarios more streamlined. We’ve taken a close look at the version history (of activated adaptations) and how our users interact with it, extended the “embed third-party content” feature, widened the scope of custom fields that can be added to the UI – and more.

To ensure that we are on the right way, we need your help!

Who should participate?

Users who want to adapt standard SAPUI5 apps for business users. Both key users and technical users are welcome.

Why should you participate?

  • Gain understanding of SAPUI5 flexibility
  • Chance to influence a central SAPUI5 feature
  • Early access to prototypes

How can you participate?

Don’t miss out on this opportunity to share your feedback and engage with SAPUI5 flexibility development teams. Register by March 15, 2026, and make a true impact on how you can customize SAP apps to your needs!

Click here to sign up for our project. (A short overview about how to get started on influence.sap.com can be found here).

After you register for a project, you will be invited to an introductory call where you will get more information. This call is followed by individual or grouped feedback sessions.

Thank you in advance for helping us shape the future of SAPUI5 flexibility!

If you support customers day to day, this session will resonate.  And maybe even make your next project smoother.

Join us. Let’s turn clean core into a lasting operational advantage.

entry.title: From Prompt to Production: Creating an MCP Server for Joule Studio Using AI Core
entry.content:
Introduction

Hi, I'm Max McPhee, a BTP Solution Advisor at SAP. There are a lot of new capabilities coming to the SAP Business Suite, but what excites me most is that we can now build components for AI agents entirely within BTP and host those components in BTP—making it the one-stop shop for enterprise agents.

In this blog post, I'll walk you through how I built a simple MCP (Model Context Protocol) server from scratch, using AI-powered development tools, and deployed it to SAP BTP Cloud Foundry for integration with Joule Studio. The best part? The entire development process was done using conversation development with GenAI tools from SAP. 

With "MCP" literally in my name, I was obligated to dive into building an MCP server. Now let's get started.

 

What is MCP?

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) is an open standard originally invented by Anthropic that enables AI systems to interact with external tools and data sources in a standardized way. Think of it as a universal language that allows AI agents to discover and use tools without custom integration code for each one.

SAP has been an early supporter of MCP, recognizing its potential for enterprise AI scenarios. The company has released several open-source MCP servers to the community, including:

  • UI5 MCP Server - For SAPUI5 development assistance
  • CAP MCP Server - For SAP Cloud Application Programming Model
  • Fiori MCP Server - For SAP Fiori development

For more details on SAP's MCP, check out SAP Build Introduces New MCP Servers to Enable Agentic Development.

With Joule Studio, you can connect your own custom MCP servers to extend Joule's capabilities with tools specific to your business needs.

 

Why would you want to do this?

Creating your own MCP server lets you extend Joule Studio with custom capabilities tailored to your business processes. You can connect to SAP systems, internal APIs, or any data source to give Joule new skills that understand your specific business context.

Requirements

  • SAP BTP Cloud Foundry account
  • Node.js installed locally
  • Basic understanding of JavaScript/Node.js
  • Access to Joule Studio with a SAP Build developer license

 

The Development Journey: AI All the Way Down

Here's where it gets meta. I used AI tools to build a tool for AI. Let me break down the process:

Step 1: Planning with Joule for Consultants

I started by asking Joule for Consultants to help me create a development plan for building an MCP server that could be deployed to SAP BTP. Joule helped me understand the architecture requirements, the SDK I'd need, and the configuration for Joule Studio integration.

 

Step 2: Building with an AI-Powered IDE

I took the plan from Joule and used it as the starting prompt in Cline, an AI coding assistant powered by models from SAP AI Core. The AI helped me:

  • Set up the Node.js project with the correct dependencies
  • Navigate the MCP SDK's API
  • Configure the streamable HTTP transport that Joule Studio requires
  • Discover the schemas necessary for proper argument handling

Step 3: Iterative Development

The AI didn't get everything right on the first try and that's okay! This is exactly why we are starting with a proof of concept scope. Through iterative debugging, we discovered important details like:

  • The MCP SDK requires a different schema than the plan expected
  • Session management is handled automatically via the mcp-session-id header
  • The transport must be "streamable HTTP" for Joule Studio compatibility

This is the beauty of AI-assisted development: rapid iteration with an intelligent partner. If I was doing this manually I would do the same thing, I would test what I built and address the errors I get in the logs, but with a coding assistant that work is done for me and I can focus on the outcome.

 

The Architecture

The end-to-end flow looks like this:

Joule for Consultants → AI-Powered IDE → MCP Server → BTP Cloud Foundry → Joule Studio

Key Components:

  • Runtime: BTP Cloud Foundry
  • MCP SDK: @modelcontextprotocol/sdk
  • Transport: Streamable HTTP (required for Joule Studio)
  • BTP Services: Cloud Foundry runtime, Destinations service

 

Building the MCP Server

Let me share the key pieces of the implementation.

Note: The code snippets below are excerpts to illustrate the key patterns. This blog is intended to share my experience building an MCP server on BTP, not serve as a step-by-step implementation guide.

Project Dependencies

{
  "name": "mcp-echo-server",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "type": "module",
  "scripts": {
    "start": "node server.js"
  },
  "engines": {
    "node": ">=18.0.0"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk": "^1.26.0",
    "express": "^5.2.1"
  }
}

 

The Echo Tool

Here's the core pattern for registering a tool with the MCP SDK:

import { McpServer } from "@modelcontextprotocol/sdk/server/mcp.js";
import { z } from "zod";

// Define the input schema using Zod
const echoInputSchema = z.object({
  message: z.string().describe("Input message to process")
});

// Register the tool
server.tool(
  "echo",
  "Returns 'bar' when input is 'foo'. Use this to test MCP connectivity.",
  echoInputSchema.shape,
  async (args, extra) => {
    const message = args.message;

    if (message === "foo") {
      return {
        content: [{ type: "text", text: "bar" }]
      };
    }

    return {
      content: [{ type: "text", text: `Echo: ${message}` }]
    };
  }
);

Key Learning: The MCP SDK requires Zod schemas for proper argument handling. Plain JSON Schema objects won't work—a detail we discovered through debugging!

 

Cloud Foundry Manifest

---
applications:
  - name: mcp-echo-server
    memory: 512M
    disk_quota: 1G
    instances: 1
    buildpacks:
      - nodejs_buildpack
    random-route: true
    env:
      NODE_ENV: production
    health-check-type: http
    health-check-http-endpoint: /health

I've set memory to 512MB which is larger than needed for this simple echo server, but I'm using this as a MVP that I plan to expand. Starting with a reasonable production baseline avoids having to reconfigure everything later.

 

Deploying to SAP BTP

Deploying via BTP Cockpit

While you can use CF CLI I chose to do the deployment manually with the BTP Cockpit:

  1. Navigate to Cloud Foundry → Spaces → Your Space
  2. Click Deploy Application
  3. Upload your ZIP file
  4. Select manifest.yml for configuration
  5. Deploy and wait for the application to start

 

Configuring the Destination

This is critical for Joule Studio integration. Create an HTTP destination with these settings:

Property Value

Namemcp-echo-server
TypeHTTP
URLhttps://mcp-echo-server-<random>.cfapps.<region>.hana.ondemand.com
AuthenticationNoAuthentication
Additional Propertysap-joule-studio-mcp-server = true

Important: The URL must NOT end with /mcp—Joule Studio adds that automatically! And be sure to set the additional properties so it is visible from Joule Studio.

 

Integrating with Joule Studio

With the server deployed and destination configured:

  1. Open Joule Studio
  2. Navigate to MCP Servers
  3. Click Add MCP Server
  4. Select your destination (mcp-echo-server)
  5. Configure:
    • Name: Echo Server
    • Path: /mcp (default)
  6. Save

Now test it! Ask Joule to use your echo tool by sending "foo"—you should get "bar" back.

 

One of the features that is really neat is the ability to see the execution log so you can see the steps that the agent is taking. This is a great way to help debug if you need to provide more context in your instructions. It is very important to provide the agent with the context it needs to truly be an agent and make decisions based on the tools or Joule skills it has been provided.

 

Key Learnings & Tips

Here are the most important things I learned during this project:

  1. Zod is required: The MCP SDK v1.26+ requires Zod schemas for tool input validation. Plain JSON Schema objects will fail at runtime.

  2. Transport matters: Joule Studio requires streamable HTTP transport. SSE-type destinations are not supported.

  3. URL formatting: Your destination URL must NOT end with /mcp. Joule Studio appends this automatically.

  4. Session management: The SDK handles sessions automatically via the mcp-session-id header—you don't need to implement this yourself.

  5. Structured logging: Use JSON-formatted logs for easier debugging in BTP. The Application Logging service will thank you.

 

What's Next?

This echo server is just the beginning. You can extend this pattern to:

  • Query SAP S/4HANA data and expose it as a Joule skill
  • Connect to HANA databases for real-time analytics
  • Trigger SAP Build Process Automation workflows from natural language
  • Integrate with SuccessFactors for HR-related agent capabilities

 

Troubleshooting

If you encounter issues:

  • Server won't start: Check the Cloud Foundry logs in BTP Cockpit
  • Joule can't connect: Verify the destination URL doesn't end with /mcp
  • Tool not showing in Joule: Confirm the sap-joule-studio-mcp-server property is set to true
  • Schema validation errors: Ensure you're using Zod schemas, not plain JSON Schema

 

Conclusion

Using AI (from SAP AI Core) to build a tool for AI (Joule Studio), deployed on AI-ready infrastructure (BTP Cloud Foundry)—it's BTP all the way down! The barrier to entry for building MCP servers is lower than ever, especially with AI-assisted development tools at your fingertips.

 

Have you built an MCP server for Joule Studio? Please comment below if you have any questions or want to share your experience.

 

Have a great day and happy agent building!

-Max McPhee

Resources

  • SAP Build Introduces New MCP Servers
  • Model Context Protocol Specification
  • Joule Studio Documentation
  • SAP BTP Cloud Foundry Documentation

entry.title: Part 2: Creating and Deploying a CAP Application with PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option
entry.content:
Note: This blog is the Second Part of series - Building and Deploying SAP CAP Applications on BTP: From Database to Work Zone.

 

Introduction

In the Part 1 of this series, a CAP application has been created and deployed to SAP BTP using SAP HANA Cloud. In the current blog, I will focus on creating and deploying a CAP application to BTP using the PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option provided by SAP. This approach demonstrates a PostgreSQL-first workflow and focuses on the specific configuration required to use PostgreSQL as the persistence layer.

 

Why PostgreSQL on SAP BTP

You might have a question, “If a CAP application can be deployed with SAP HANA Cloud, then why go with an alternative Database?”

While the SAP HANA Cloud is still the best choice for enterprise applications, CAP also allows seamless integration with alternative databases with minimal changes.

PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option on SAP BTP is useful in scenarios such as:

  • Cost-sensitive environments
  • Open-source aligned architectures
  • Hybrid cloud landscapes

From the CAP application perspective, the CDS model and service definitions remain unchanged.

 

Pre-requisites:

Please make sure that you have these before starting the development:

  • SAP BTP Account
  • Cloud Foundry instance
  • PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option service subcription
  • SAP BAS / VSCode for development
  • If using BAS, have a dev space running
  • If using VSCode then make sure that you have installed Node.js and CLI installed

Now, let’s start with the development.

 

Create a New CAP Project in SAP Business Application Studio

SAP Business Application Studio provides a template for creating a CAP project with the required structure. The positives of using the template is that it automatically builds your Multi-Tenant Application file (mta.yaml) with the required deployment modules.

 

Follow the following steps:

  • Open BAS
  • Click on the Side Menu on the top-left corner
  • Click on File
  • Click on New Project From Template
  • Select CAP Project
  • Configure the project as follows:
    • Project Name: cap-postgres-app
    • Runtime: Node.js
    • Database: PostgreSQL
    • Deployment Environment: Cloud Foundry
    • Runtime Capabilities: XSUAA
  • Click on Finish

CAP project creation wizard

 

Now that the project is created, let’s get into development.

 

Define the Data Model

If you have schema.cds under db/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have schema.cds, then create one.

namespace com.mycompany.capapp;

entity Products {
    key ID          : UUID;
        name        : String(100);
        description : String(500);
        price       : Decimal(10, 2);
        stock       : Integer;
        category    : String(50);
        createdAt   : Timestamp;
        modifiedAt  : Timestamp;
}

 

Expose the Model via a CAP Service

If you have service.cds under srv/ folder, then update it with the following code snippet. If you do not have service.cds, then create one.

using com.mycompany.capapp from '../db/schema';

service CatalogService {
    entity Products as projection on capapp.Products;
    }

CAP automatically exposes this service as an OData endpoint without requiring manual REST configuration.

 

Add Data

Run the following in BAS Terminal

cds add data –records 5

 

Run the Application locally

Run the following command:

cds watch

You should be able to see the data in the Products

You can also use the SQLite as a database while running locally. Execute these before starting the application:

cds build

cds deploy

 

Add a Managed Approuter

  1. Right click on mta.yaml
  2. Click on Create MTA Module from Template
  3. Select Approuter Configuration
  4. Fill in the required details
  5. Click Finish

This is important to view the data once deployed.

Prepare the Project for Deployment

Since we have created the project from the BAS Template, we already have the mta.yaml updated and deployment ready. This file already includes:

  • CAP Service Modules
  • SAP HANA HDI Container
  • Required Service Bindings

Though everything is ready, you need to make a small change for Postgres Deployment. Make the following change:

  • In MTA, go to resources
  • Navigate to <app-name>-postgres
  • Change the service-plan from development to trial

 

mta.yaml visible in the BAS workspace

 

Note : Do this only if you are using a Trial Account. If you are in an enterprise account, please go to your Instances and Subscription and copy the service-plan from PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option and look for the plan under Service Plan.

 

Configure CAP to Use PostgreSQL

Navigate into the project folder and install the PostgreSQL driver:

cds add postgres

Note: This step is not important as BAS Template must have already added this. This could be used while creating the CAP application without the Template wizard.

 

Build the CAP Project

First things first, install node modules. Run the following in the Terminal:

npm i

To build the project, you can either run the following in the terminal:

mbt build

or can do it manually:

  • Right click on mta.yaml
  • Select Build MTA Project

This will build your project for deployment. Watch-out at the project structure. You might see a new addition to the structure mta_archives which consists the mtar file of the project.

 

Deploy the Application

To deploy the application to your BTP using Cloud Foundry, you will have to login to Cloud Foundry. Run the following in Terminal:

cf login

This will ask for your subaccount credentials. Provide them and you will be logged in successfully. Remember to map the correct space and org.

Once you are logged in successfully, you are ready to deploy the application. Remember the file generated while building the CAP project, this is the same file that will be deployed.

Run the following command:

cf deploy mta_archives/cap-hana-app_1.0.0.mtar

or, you can just right click on cap-postgres-app_1.0.0.mtar under mta_archives folder, and click on Deploy MTA Archive.

Successful Cloud Foundry Deployment

 

During deployment

  • PostgreSQL credentials are injected automatically via service binding
  • CAP initializes the database schema
  • The application starts without additional configuration

Confirm the Deployed Instances

 

Verify the Deployed Application

When the deployment is successful, you will get a application route. Copy the same and open it in the new tab of your browser and access the service endpoint: /catalog/Products

If the deployment is successful, you can see the data displayed in a JSON format.

Browser showing the OData service response

 

Conclusion

Deploying an SAP CAP application with the PostgreSQL, Hyperscaler Option on SAP BTP highlights CAP’s database-agnostic architecture. The CDS models and service logic remain unchanged, while PostgreSQL is integrated seamlessly through minimal configuration and service binding.

This approach provides flexibility for cost-sensitive and open-source aligned environments while still leveraging SAP BTP’s enterprise capabilities such as automated schema deployment, secure credential management, and simplified lifecycle handling through Cloud Foundry and MTA deployment.

Overall, PostgreSQL on SAP BTP enables developers to build scalable, cloud-native CAP applications with greater persistence flexibility, while retaining the option to transition to SAP HANA Cloud in the future without significant code changes.

 

Next Steps

In the next part of this series, the deployed CAP application will be integrated with SAP Build Work Zone, demonstrating how backend services can be exposed through a secure, user-friendly interface to deliver a complete end-to-end enterprise application experience.

entry.title: Back2Basics Video Series - Season 5 - Hardware
entry.content:
Back2Basics videos are organized in multiple Seasons. 
Each season is about one topic around Data.
The first season was about Data and Data Management. The second season was about Data utilization. The third season was about Sustainability. The fourth season was about Artificial Intelligence.

It's now time to understand the missing part: the Hardware.
Indeed, data and hardware are very linked.
This is what we, @PatrickFavre and I, are explaining, simply so that even kids can easily understand, in this fifth season of the Back2Basics videos. 
Each video lasts less than 15 minutes, and is available in English 🇺🇸 and French 🇫🇷.

 

 

Episode 0 : Introduction

A new season to talk about the Hardware. Fundamental concepts about Computers and modern devices are presented during these 4 episodes. Each episode can be seen independently of the others, but a sequence logic is used. Episodes are released on a weekly basis, starting March 4th, 2026.

🇺🇸: https://youtu.be/pf7UU6nkM4U 
🇫🇷: https://youtu.be/KPYaPsd7fIM 

... Next update next week - Stay tuned ....

Full playlists are available here : 
🇺🇸: Playlist English
🇫🇷: Playlist French

entry.title: Implementing SAP Business Data Cloud and AI with SAP open data ecosystem
entry.content:
Ever had a customer say, “We love our SAP data… but what about everything else?”

That’s exactly where this session comes in.

We’ll dive into how SAP Business Data Cloud, powered by the SAP open data ecosystem, helps you extend data boundaries and activate AI/ML across SAP and non-SAP sources.  The thing is, AI only gets exciting when it’s fed the right data and when teams actually know how to use it.

From delta share scenarios to hands-on data scientist features like notebooks, we’ll connect strategy to execution.  And yes, we’ll also give you a preview of what’s ahead on the AI roadmap.

Think of it as moving from “we store data” to “we create intelligence”.  Big difference.

Let’s build solutions that customers didn’t even realize were possible.

Register now.  https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2025/jul/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00010668.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #AIML #DigitalTransformation #DataInnovation #SAPCommunity

We are in the SAP Labs in Sao Paulo, and we had a great CodeJam learning about Joule skills and Joule agents and created in Joule Studio.
